title: Opening Basics
subtitle: "The opening is the phase where you bring your pieces out, not where you try to win the game."

blocks.html:
The Four Opening Goals (In Priority Order)

  1. Control the center — Am I occupying or threatening e4/d4 (White) or e5/d5 (Black)?
  2. Develop pieces — How many pieces are still on the back rank?
  3. Castle to safety — Is my king castled by move 10?
  4. Connect rooks — After castling + queen moves, are my rooks connected?

The Development Race

After move 8: 4+ pieces developed. After move 12: all minor pieces developed + king castled.
For White, you need ONE opening. The Italian Game is ideal — follows all principles, develops naturally, and teaches sound chess.

blocks.caption: The Italian Game: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Bc5 4.c3 (preparing d4). White develops naturally, targets f7, and prepares to seize the center.

blocks.html:
Key ideas:

  • Bc4 targets f7 — the weakest square in Black's camp
  • c3 followed by d4 — the "classical center" formation
  • Nf3 before Nc3 — defends e4 and eyes the center

If Black plays 3...Nf6 (Two Knights Defense), White can launch the Fried Liver Attack: 4.Ng5 d5 5.exd5 Nxd5 6.Nxf7!

Option A: 1...e5 (Classical)

Mirror White's good moves. Solid and sound. After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6, you're in standard territory.

Option B: Scandinavian Defense (Easy to Learn)

1.e4 d5 — immediately challenge the center!

blocks.caption: Scandinavian Defense: 1.e4 d5 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3 Qa5. Black challenges the center immediately. The queen retreats to safety on a5, and Black develops normally from there.

blocks.html:
Pros: Highly forcing, easy to understand positions.
Cons: Queen moves early (violates a principle), but it's safe here.

Tempo (plural: tempi) = a unit of time in chess. One move = one tempo. The player who wastes fewer tempi develops faster.

Gaining a Tempo

You "gain a tempo" when you develop a piece while simultaneously making a threat that forces the opponent to waste a move.

The Principle: Develop With Threat

The ideal opening move develops a piece AND creates a threat. This is the most efficient use of a tempo.

Scholar's Mate Defense

After 1.e4 e5 2.Bc4 Nc6 3.Qh5 — DON'T play 3...Nf6?? (4.Qxf7#). Instead: ...Qe7, ...Qf6, or ...g6.

blocks.caption: Scholar's Mate attempt: 1.e4 e5 2.Bc4 Nc6 3.Qh5 Nf6?? 4.Qxf7#. Don't fall for this! After 3.Qh5, play 3...Qe7, ...g6, or ...Qf6.

blocks.html:
Legal's Mate

White sacrifices the queen for checkmate. 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 d6 3.Bc4 Bg4 4.Nc3 g6? 5.Nxe5!! Bxd1?? 6.Bxf7+ Ke7 7.Nd5#

Kingside (O-O) vs. Queenside (O-O-O)

KingsideQueenside
Faster (only N+B to move)Slower (Q-side pieces to move)
King safer (3 pawns)King somewhat exposed
Default choiceAggressive choice
95% of gamesUse only when you know why

Recommendation: Castle kingside in 95% of games until ELO 1000+.

After castling, the pawns on f2/g2/h2 (or f7/g7/h7) are your king's shield. Every pawn you push weakens this shield.

After Development: Form a Plan

  1. Where are the weaknesses? (Backward pawns, open files, weak squares)
  2. Which side to play on? (Where you have more space/pieces)
  3. What's my worst piece? (Improve it!)
  4. Can I create a threat? (CCT scan!)

The Rook Question

Rooks belong on open files (no pawns) or semi-open files (only enemy pawns). Don't leave rooks sitting in the corner.

checkpoint: Can you play the first 8 moves of the Italian Game from memory? Do you know how to defend against Scholar's Mate? Do you castle kingside by move 10? If yes, proceed to Chapter 5.
summary:
ConceptKey Point
White openingItalian Game (1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4)
Black opening1...e5 (classical) or 1...d5 (Scandinavian)
TempoDevelop with threat whenever possible
CastlingKingside, by move 10
TrapsKnow Scholar's Mate and how to defend
RooksPlace on open files
